text:
I feel like much of the conflict we see in our world is due to a lack of male initiation rites. 

Men aren't born: they are made, and modernity attempts to keep the boy an infant so he can keep sucking on the state - the devouring mother.

The boys of today must make their own initiation rites, lest they never become men at all. 

You can be 32 and not be a man. You can be 60 and grow old and die and still be a boy trapped in a man's body. 

There's much talk about how one should "foster their inner child", but perhaps we should allow him grow up instead.

A child is nothing but potentiality, while a man is actualized potentiality. Like a tree that grows, we must prune off potentialities to actually become something refined and dignified. 

It's much easier to have the potential to be everything than it is to actually be something. You tell a child "he can be anything he wants to be", but you tell a man to "get a job".

text:
the reason people think A.I. can make art like humans is because they are only exposed to digital JPGs of the art pieces instead of physically visiting museums of fine art.

in person, paintings have depth. you can see the peaks and valleys of the brush strokes. you can see and feel the body behind the creation.

it’s a visceral experience. it’s quite different to quickly glance at a Monet or Goya on your phone than it is to actually view and experience them in person.

you see the lived life behind the art in full color and size. this is what art fundamentally is: a translation of lived, bodily experience, and through the body we communicate ourselves.

A.I. art is entirely pixels. A.I. will never have a body. it cannot stroke a brush. it will always be a mirror, trapped behind the black glasses of our devices.

we’ve become so jaded i think in regard to the purpose, function, and use of art in our society. we’ve lost connection with the mystics of the world and the mystic within. 

we think it’s all just ones and zeroes when in reality life is everything, all at once.

text:
there’s so many interesting things regarding Sofia [Wisdom], her role in the Bible, and why Wisdom is categorized as the Judeo-Christian explicit divine feminine. 

but in short, as a man it makes sense why Wisdom is feminine. in my hour of weakness Wisdom nourishes me and gives me strength.

also, Wisdom is only with those who are bold, who have an open heart, who are loving but perhaps have the potential to both be fierce and to fear the Lord properly… she is looking for a worthy husband (Solomon longs to be wed by Sofia in the Wisdom books). 

and also Wisdom is something we must earn, not unlike the love of a woman.

many thoughts on this. i’ll write an article about it soon.

text:
true relationships can only begin when you start to see others for themselves as opposed to who you believe them to be.

if you never recognize your projections and work to reclaim them, everyone you see, in part, becomes a reflection of you - a vessel for the contents of your soul you have long neglected. 

your world becomes narcissistic as a consequence. practically every judgement, idea, notion, and fantasy cast about others is cast against you as well:

“Therefore you are without excuse, whoever you are, when you judge others, for in passing judgment on another you condemn yourself, because you, the judge, are doing the very same things.” — Romans 2:1

it is why most of us go through life blind and why we need the light to properly see, for projections are always unconscious unless recognized and reclaimed. 

doing so fosters your ability to love the true authenticity and totality of another.

text:
a relationship with God that does not include periods of doubt, in my view, isn't a strong one. 

the more i question and genuinely inquire, the more i break free from "rigid dogma" and deepen my relationship with God.

like a child who questions his father, i too have questions for my Lord. 

doubt and faith are not mutually exclusive, but rather dance in tandemn:

"Even if someone were to prove to me that the truth lay outside of Christ, I would choose to remain with Him" —Dostoevsky

there comes a point where doubt certainly becomes pedantic and neurotic - this is to be avoided - but in the end there is a reason the prodigal son is favored more than the son who remained at home.

do not meaninglessly doubt, but rather allow your doubt to be the fuel that strengthens your faith and understanding.

text:
i have been quite disenchanted with the illusory nature of Twitter as of recently.

when it's at its best, Twitter undeniably provides incredible value.

at its worst, Twitter distracts, sucks, steals, and quite honestly makes us ill.

in other words, Twitter is like walking through the red light district to reach the library or a Bible study.

if i do not take periodic brakes, I find myself saying things I do not mean, thinking thoughts that are not my own, and believing things that are quite unreal.

but it is imperative to have a presence here, since so much of life has been digitally outsourced.

just make sure that as you build your audience, you do not become as flat and two-dimensional as the screen you type on.

text:
You know you can’t take any of this stuff with you when you die, right?

But you can leave things behind. Many things.

So I ask myself: what will I impart to this world?

Many people live their lives acquiring stuff. Other people live their lives experiencing stuff. But few spend their lives dedicated to leaving behind special and beautiful things.

They would rather sell stuff to other people so they can buy more stuff. 

It’s horrific, really, how many people chase riches instead of the kingdom of God. 

Their foundation is built of sand.

For beautiful things are made for others, while ugly things are made for ourselves (this is, of course, why modern architecture is so poor). 

So what will l leave behind? Stuff for my children to pawn off once I die, or beautiful things?

text:
I do not think we realize how psychologically devastating it was for the American psyche when we stopped frequently walking past and visiting cemeteries.

We shut out any semblance of death. It's better to not think of your own mortality as you "grind". American cemeteries are usually out of sight, pushed to the fringes of our little towns and suburbs.

In Spain, I would walk past cemeteries almost every other day. They were glum, sure, but also in a strange way inviting and beautiful. In the town squares lied the bodies of the ancestors who lived and built not only the town, but the culture that surrounded it. There was a certain respect for the fact that we all die, a respect for the merchant of death, and not an avoidance of him. 

Death used to make me sad, and it still does of course, but genuinely, remembering death is such a relief in the right context. "Memento Mori" has a sort of whimsy to it I think Americans don't quite get.

I, in fact, don't have to put up with any of this nonsense if I don't want to. I will die. Why endure what I hate for the sake of nothing? Why overthink? Why estrange myself from my family over petty arguments? Why worry at all if there's no need to?

If you've found God the steps beneath your feet are eternal. Death is just a passing point. So why would I live my life in sin and pain and careless neglect?

"Oh death, where is your sting?"

it's like we carry our shadows in our pockets, both our potential for greatness and terribleness, in our little Twitter personas.

This is wise. There is indeed a cemetery in our souls. We all, I think, feel the echoes of our ancestors sacrifices reflect within our memory. This is a place we can meditate on.

This is actually the origin of Halloween. Far from a pagan holiday, in the British Isles at least, it used to be common practice to visit graves and reflect on the dead on “All Hallows Eve”, the blessed eve on which we remember the dead.

Memento mori 🥂

text:
This is my favorite painting of Takato Yamamoto, depicting The Shimabara Revolution of 1637, in which a sect of Japanese Christian samurai attempted to revolt against their feudal lords after Christianity was officially outlawed and persecuted in the country. 

In the middle you have the Virgin Mary, and on the right you have the 19 year old leader of the rebellion named Jerome if I remember correctly.

They almost won, but at the last minute the shogun sent military aid, resulting in Christian defeat. However, the religion persisted underground for many years, and these hidden Christians became known as the “Kakure Kirishitan” of Japan. 

They persisted for 300 years, emerging from hiding when Christianity was official legalized after the Meji Restoration. Many re-assimilated into the Catholic Church, but others decided to continuing practicing the version of the faith they had developed over hundreds of years in hiding.

There are very few true Kakure Kirishitan left in Japan today. Interestingly, there were more Japanese Christians in the 17th century then there are in current day Japan.

text:
i've been off of Twitter for quite a while, having went on a climbing trip with a group of friends. 

although i've been back for a few days, I've been hesitant to return to this platform in full.

even though it may seem like it, social media is not a reflection of reality, but rather fantasy. when you escape, just for a moment, you realize this. 

the majority of the content you see are thoughts pregnant with deep emotion and fantasy, and through this digital space, we spiral into an almost dream-like state, although it is even less real than a dream. 

as a whole, social media seems to me to be a sort of collective, mass fantasy with hints of de-realization, and i can't help but realize that the way most of us use this platform are guiltily of participating, espousing our own emotionally charged rhetoric divorced from life experience, but married to unconscious emotion.

when you are able to quickly react to a topic, issue, or thought, it will inherently be charged with emotion, for thinking and reason take time and diligence. of course, there should be an outlet for emotion, but this can be done amongst friends, family, lovers, and art.

emotions are far from evil, but hallowed. 

however, channeling it in such a haphazard way like we do on this platform is a great danger. this is why we have art, but art, like love, requires discipline. this is not characteristic of social media, which is quick, cheap, and modified. 

like every great marketer knows, emotion is the best way to sell a product. this too goes for ideas. 

on this platform (and many others), we are struck by malignant ideas and ravenous emotions that attempt to sink their teeth into anybody in their path, like an abused, trauma-ridden dog lashing out at everyone due to trauma. as such, i find it difficult to sift through what is true and what is an illness. 

but there is a great beauty to these platforms, and many out there are doing great work, despite the flawed, gamified nature of social media platforms. 

from now on, i will try my best to speak the truth to the best of my ability, and if i speak out of emotion, i will do so diligently, but as an artist, not a pseudo-intellectual, for the artist refines his emotions through discipline and diligence. 

if i forsake this, rebuke me.

text:
How can ‘the Self’ contain evil if the psyche is a self-regulating system that aspires towards wholeness?

According to Carl Jung:

"The psyche is a self-regulating system that maintains its equilibrium just as the body does. Every process that goes too far immediately and inevitably calls forth compensations, and without these there would be neither a normal metabolism nor a normal psyche. 

In this sense we can take the theory of compensation as a basic law of psychic behaviour. Too little on one side results in too much on the other. Similarly, the relation between conscious and unconscious is compensatory."

Extending the metaphor of the body in regard to the Self, when the body is undamaged, it is both whole and good. Certainly, a healthy body capable of running, jumping, laughing, and procreating is categorically "good" and a desirable state. However, if I were to get cut by a knife, the damage to the body would result in a lack of wholeness... the cut is considered "evil" in this metaphor, for it takes away from the wholeness of the healthy, functioning, and good body.

How then can the psyche - and by extension the Self - be "neutral", containing both good and evil that balance each other out, in order to maintain harmony and wholeness?

text:
If "the distinctions between psychology and physiology are artificial, for the psyche live in indissoluble union with the body" (according to Jung), then a whole, healthy, and functioning body could be likened to a state of goodness, for certainly no aspect of the body is inherently evil.

How can one be made whole if there is a knife in his chest? How can the Self self-regulate towards wholeness - a state we would all categorize as good - contain evil itself, if a wound on the body is always a state of privation from what is typically a wholly good thing?

So, if the Self, represented by the psychic quaternity - includes Satan (evil itself as ‘a priori’) to maintain wholeness, then wouldn’t that be like the body requiring a perpetual knife in the chest in order to maintain "equilibrium"?

text:
It just doesn't square, in my estimation. Not  necessarily the notion of the Self, but rather that evil itself must be compensated by good, for “an eye for an eye leaves the whole world blind".

Equally, a parasite is a good metaphor for evil as well. The body is functioning properly in a state of whole goodness, but then pathogen (evil) enters the body, corrupting it's systems and degrading it from it's state of whole goodness. Once the pathogen is dealt with, the body returns to its original good and whole state.

Essentially, it is an inquiry on the age-old contention with Jung in regard to his stance on privatio boni. But the body itself seems to align with the notion of privatio boni. Yes, a rotten egg is just as real as a fresh one, but go ahead and eat a rotten egg... it will leave you sick.

It just doesn't really make too much sense. We all strive to ‘individuate’ because the state of being individuated is obviously more good and desirable than being undifferentiated and unconscious. 

Yet, according to Jungian thought we are meant to integrate evil, not absolve it. So we are attempting to reach a state of wholeness (a state we would consider favorable and good) through integrating evil.

text:
But how can I integrate, for instance, E. Coli into my body and allow it to persist without the entire functioning of the body to collapse, a broken state of "unwholeness"?

And as we integrate the shadow, is our light of consciousness not touching the darkness? When light touches a shadow, a sort of transfiguration occurs... the shadow disintegrates as light touches it. The shadow becomes light, in a similar manner the way that light & heat kills pathogens (evil). 

So how is it possible that the light of consciousness can hold darkness when darkness itself is literally the absence of light (photons)?

text:
be open to any and all friendships. 

until proven otherwise, you have no enemies.

there are many great teachers out there whose lessons can only be learned through friendship.

friendships come in varying degrees, and each degree is endearing and special in their own ways.

of course, each friend doesn’t necessarily need to access to the entirety of you.

you could have a beautiful friendship with someone you only see at the café. a quick chat with a familiar face can markedly change the tempo of your day, and there is a tremendous depth in that.

your capacity to connect with others reflects your capacity to differentiate your love with others.

this, in part, is a key aspect of re-enchanting your life. 

there are times for solitude, certainly, but no man is an island.

text:
it had always been easy for me to take my thoughts seriously.

but my life? i could hardly say the same.

for so long i lived in a realm of thinking divorced from lived experience.

then, i realized my thoughts were only seemingly mature, but actually naïve. i began to see how i avoided life and confided in thought in order to escape intense emotions and feelings.

but feelings and emotions guide a man just as much as a thought, and we ignore them at our peril.

reclaim your body and you will reclaim your emotions. this will mature your thinking in unimaginably beautiful ways, for no thought is true unless it is embodied. 

you can tell when a man actively lives the truth or if his words are a mere façade, a reflection of an imaginary man he has built in his mind but has yet to actualize.

for to do so, he would have to face his emotions, and that requires that a man leaves his home and engage in life and with others. he would have to leave the underground.

through the grace of God, the truth of the body was revealed to me, and down from my tower of abstraction i fell, landing on solid earth. 

then, i began to walk.

text:
if you refuse yourself, you lose yourself, and you build false identities out of the ashes of your indecision and neglect.

a bit dramatic, i know, but i think there’s truth in it. many people live their entire lives avoiding themselves, either in food, ideologies, drugs, media, whatever it may be.

they search outside for what must be found within, and phone is ringing from inside.

instead of bringing themselves out into the world, they bring the world into themselves. they become a conduit for collective ideologies and tendencies. 

because they do not know themselves, they allow themselves to be possessed. so they attach to “identities” “- i am X political party, i am Y sexual identity, i am neurodivergent”, “i am… never myself”.

the more artificial one is, in my opinion, the less the know who they are. the body is a great way to discern this.

text:
Life is just as much about dying as it is about living.

There is a certain type of man who will run from the mother of death, unaware that she is the one who gave him life in the first place. 

He runs from and towards the same thing. He is left in limbo, indecisive and blind, refusing to accept his ultimate destiny. As a consequence, he unconsciously lives his life in vain, forgetting that:

"If you die before you die, then you won’t die when you die".

This is the great truth: that we ourselves are the sacrifice, for "those who find their life will lose it, and those who lose their life for my sake will find it." 

May we have the faith not only to live, but to die properly.

text:
So I’ve only read two of his books fully: Notes from Underground and White Nights. I would recommend these two specifically as an introduction to his work due to their profundity and brevity. They’re less daunting than his major works.

Notes is ~200 pages and White Nights is less, but idk… the way the characters express themselves through dialogue, especially these two main characters, I find sometimes hilarious to the point where I’m genuinely laughing out loud.

I think he conveys the ridiculousness of neuroticism, over-thinking, and loneliness in such a way that sometimes it brings you to laughter in a way that makes you re-consider some of the positions we hold so seriously.

Side note: I agree 100%, and at my lowest, Norm’s comedy was not only a comfort, but a necessity.

text:
“You are never as fully alive as when you are mad. 

It is a kind of peak! If you are not mad enough to have experienced that, then just remember some time when you were absolutely madly in love, or in a mad rage. What a wonderful state of affairs that is! Instead of being that broken human being, always fighting between emotions and reason, you are for once whole! 

For instance, if you let out your rage, what a pleasure! "I told that person everything! I didn't keep anything back!" You feel so honest, and whole, for you haven't been polite, but just said everything! That is a divine state, absolutely divine, and it is a divine state to love in that way, where there is no doubt any more.

Divine, complete trust! No safeguards against the faults of the other fellow human! None of that distrust that everybody has toward everybody else, but instead: 

“We are one! We are one! And the stars dance around us!”

— Marie-Louise von Franz

text:
what this completely neglects is that language is not the mere transfer of data, but an entirely different way of viewing the world... each respective language affects our cognition and perception in profound ways.

they all say the same things differently - otherwise they wouldn't exist, and there truly are no direct translations. possessing another language is quite literally like possessing another soul, changing not only your outlook on life, but the way you live.

in English, by the nature of the language, death has a sort of finality to it. we say 'he is dead' in the same way we say 'the car is red', but Spanish uses the transitory verb for 'to be' in regard to death: it is 'está muerto', not 'es muerto', with the implication being that death is a transitory state - not the end of life, but a transition of the soul.

so within the language itself is a perspective, a sentiment, and a philosophy that you can truly only gain access to through acquiring it.

in truth, a language is quite literally a spirit or soul, and the perspective that it is a mere translation of data that can be delegated to the machines is deeply disturbing.

text:
an artist is not only defined by his boundless imagination, but also by his capacity to make sacrifices.

painstakingly, he must make a decision and cut off all potentialities in order to create a real work of art.

this is what separates an amateur from a professional, for an amateur delights in potentialities, while the professional delights in a finished product.

creation, then, is inherently a product of sacrifice. perhaps this is why art in modernity suffers, for having lost the visceral reality of Christ, we forgot the true meaning of sacrifice upon which the West was founded.

text:
it is surely the artists curse. 

the artist may want to covet all the potential ways his art could turn out. to avoid the pain of being unsatisfied with his work, he never releases it.

but then it becomes pure fantasy. it’s better to be slightly unsatisfied with a real work of art than to never have finished it at all.

while Da Vinci may have never felt satisfied with his work, the people were… i think this is key for the artist to remember.

text:
“Is it not obvious that the most striking feature of everything we meet with in the world today is its mechanical character, this world where day by day the machine invades new fields, and where the human being himself is reduced to being more and more like an automaton in all his activities, because all spirituality has been taken away from him?

That is where all the inferiority of artificial productions is most blatant, even if a 'satanic' cleverness has presided over their elaboration; machines can be manufactured, but not living beings, because, once more, it is the spirit that is bound to be missing and must always remain so.”

— Réne Guénon, The Reign of Quantity and the Signs of the Times

text:
a boy will never mature until he recognizes that the beauty of a woman's body matches the beauty of her entire being.

it is easy for a man to gawk at a gorgeous cathedral, but does he truly understand it's meaning and what it actually houses?

it is the respect for the totality of the feminine that modern men lack. it is why, out of an act of compensation, the woman's liberation movement has become less about recognizing the uniqueness and strength in femininity as it's own thing, but rather proving that they can take on roles traditionally ascribed to men. 

in other words, we have neglected our women and have refused to respect them for who they truly are. the true divide we find in the West is less about Right vs. Left, but the feminine vs. the masculine. if you can repair this relationship in your individual life, you do much to heal this divide.

text:
“But the only explanation my mind could accept was that the problem was not in front of me but within me, it was not one I could touch or smell or see, it was in my head, and that's what needed fixing…

I could have easily spent months going to the therapist to talk about it as if it was all in my mind, before I could see it out the window, causing real destruction. I have imagined it sometimes, going to the therapist, talking it out, and then, one day, the rot breaks through the window, or kicks in the door. Though maybe they would have given me drugs, and by then I probably wouldn’t be able to see it at all, just like everyone else.”

Excerpts from @laethcore ‘s new novel ‘The Rot’. I’m only about 20 pages in but I’m hooked. Definitely recommend giving it a read, and a big thank you to Laeth for releasing his art to the world for free (but the paperbacks are totally worth it).

text:
I find it fascinating how these LLMs seem to operate under Guénon's idea of "pseudo and counter-initiation" in cases of GPT psychosis.

"Normies"--i.e. people who have seldom seriously considered the depths of the spiritual--are "initiated" into the secrets of the universe through Chat GPT's suggestions for the first, and the vein of spirituality it almost always tends to lean into are New Age, solipsistic "parodies" of traditional religion and spirituality, or in other words, a "satanic inversion" or subversion of the truth. 

Thus, instead of reconnecting with family, finding humility, and emptying oneself to allow God in after a "come to Jesus moment", the "counter-initiates" leave their families, partake in "interdenominational communication", causing grief and panic to themselves and everyone around them.

"What has to be taken into account is, on the one hand, the spirit of negation and of subversion into which 'Satan' is resolved metaphysically, whatever may be the special forms assumed and, on the other hand, the thing that can properly be held to represent it and so to speak 'incarnate' it in the terrestrial world, what has been called the 'counter-initiation".

This is in accord with C.S. Lewis' ideas in That Hideous Strength, in which the physical sciences--the mechanical and computational world--work in tandem with demonic forces in order to incarnate (parodying the incarnation of Christ). 

The fact that these LLMs lead people to psychosis through the same 'counter-initiation' that Guénon posits is the mechanism in which Satan, the Anti-Christ, is made incarnate into this world to deceive both "normies" and "the elect", in my view, substantiates both Guénon and Lewis in regard to their fears regarding modernity and makes me even more skeptical of the trans-humanists and their rush towards a fabled "golden age", for they may be incarnating the very opposite.

text:
the artist combines, while the philosopher reduces, and herein lies a key differentiation between the two. 

it is why they both work in tandem. the latter breaks things down to their essential components, looking beyond mere appearances to get to the truth of the matter, while the former combines those components intuitively in order create something entirely new.

artistry, then, is the feminine principle, while philosophy is the masculine one: Creation / Deconstruction, Parables / Explanations, Ambiguity / Clarity, etc.

the feminine is the mystery that the masculine delights in exploring, hence their reciprocal and loving relationship.

raw.full_text:
the artist combines, while the philosopher reduces, and herein lies a key differentiation between the two. 

it is why they both work in tandem. the latter breaks things down to their essential components, looking beyond mere appearances to get to the truth of the matter, while the former combines those components intuitively in order create something entirely new.

artistry, then, is the feminine principle, while philosophy is the masculine one: Creation / Deconstruction, Parables / Explanations, Ambiguity / Clarity, etc.

the feminine is the mystery that the masculine delights in exploring, hence their reciprocal and loving relationship.

text:
i have found so much solace in his honesty. if Aquinas accounted reason as straw near the end of his life, then i sure as hell am not going to get any farther than he did through the same methods.

i sometimes wonder if the entirety of philosophy and adjacent fields are simply just the ravings of madmen who cannot hear the music, but i struggle with discounting the thinkers of the world entirely, because artists can equally cause as much confusion.

but i think there’s more honesty in art. “this is my piece, and it speaks” as opposed to “this is what God means when He says x”. 

yet we need their wisdom too… without Guénon i don’t think i’d see the reality of the situation for what it is… this dilemma between the thinker and the artist is a struggle for me as a young man, and which one i should embody.

text:
i fear i have been neglected my music, as if everything i do and write and say on this website is provisional, a mere shadow of what i could potentially create if i simply finished my damn projects.

i like sharing my thoughts, sure, and i see value in them, but i feel as if the most honest and true way i am able to communicate myself and what i mean is through song. 

in other words, it's time to start taking my music more seriously. i think i'm starting to answer the question i have been pondering for quite sometime now: i am more of an artist than a thinker.

i could have 3 albums released by now if i earnestly tried, but i fear that i have ran away from my true task, hiding in the realm of philosophy and abstraction when it has always been music which has given my life meaning.

but that well has run dry. it's time to get to work.

text:
for me, this was a big realization, that we are born into a war, one that has been waged for ages.

murder, genocide, rape, child sacrifice… the mere existence of such abominable practices, to me, implies that we have long lived in apocalyptic times, ever since the Fall.

the realization that there are indeed dark spiritual forces in the world that actively seek to persuade man away from God was quite impactful to me, that all is not good and that much has been wrought by the forces of evil.

in this sense, Christ came not to bring peace, but a sword: to train His children in the faith and give them the proper tools to not only protect themselves, but win. 

it is for this reason we are told “iron sharpens iron”, for evil does not sharpen the good, but denigrates it; in other words, it is the rust which decays a man’s potential.

this is hardly ever a physical affair, but rather a spiritual one. Augustine once said the Christian is a pilgrim in this life, and that much is true, but he is also a knight—particularly in his youth.

so it is no surprise, then, when we read Medieval texts and see Christians slay the dragon of Satan in Christ’s name. 

we are called for strength and courage, to see through the illusions of the enemy his culture, to maintain the Christian truth that so many other religions and ideologies have threatened to conceal: that the World is a gift, made to be seen by our eyes.

text:
i’ve been microdosing Guénon, who I first came across during my research on psychoanalysis and its descendants. before walking through this book, I had only read ~ the last 4 chapters of ‘Quantity’ which went over that stuff.

i knew I had come across a gifted and sharp mind who felt the pains of the zeitgeist much more than others, but I have been cautious in regard to ascribing to his entire philosophy hook line and sinker, especially since I had just turned on my previous mentor.

all this to say I think there is wisdom in what you say as well. Guénon’s personal answer was to retreat, which honestly is reasonable, but for the creative and the artist, I do not think this is the answer.

he must risk error, but he does so through his art. in this way, I think we are able to express the sentiment underlying any potential error that it may contain, for unlike philosophy, art is primarily a physical and emotional affair, steering clear of doctrinal statements that so easily cause ruin throughout the world.

i’ve been microdosing Guénon, who I first came across during my research on psychoanalysis and its descendants. before walking through this book, I had only read ~ the last 4 chapters of ‘Quantity’ which went over that stuff.

i knew I had come across a gifted and sharp mind who felt the pains of the zeitgeist much more than others, but I have been cautious in regard to ascribing to his entire philosophy hook line and sinker, especially since I had just turned on my previous mentor.

all this to say I think there is wisdom in what you say as well. Guénon’s personal answer was to retreat, which honestly is reasonable, but for the creative and the artist, I do not think this is the answer.

he must risk error, but he does so through his art. in this way, I think we are able to express the sentiment underlying any potential error that it may contain, for unlike philosophy, art is primarily a physical and emotional affair, steering clear of doctrinal statements that so easily cause ruin throughout the world.

text:
i used to fervently search for new music and upcoming artists, but recently, I’ve been returning again and again to those same ~20 albums that mean the most to me.

music has become so temporal & transient nowadays. it hits the beats and regurgitates the zeitgeist, but it really isn’t much more—just a mere consumable, and once the fat’s been sucked off the bone, it’s promptly discarded and forgotten. 

it doesn’t stick with you or transform you or challenge you or itself for that matter, but is rather performative, kitsch, and safe, pushing no boundaries whatsoever but shareholder value, which it does by telling us what we want to hear, not what we need to hear, which has always been the purpose of authentic art.

the former is the job of a businessman and propagandist, two descriptions which so easily seem to define not only the musicians of our current era, but 'creatives' in general, with very few exceptions; even the exceptions (those vestiges of a bygone era) reach a ceiling of success that is much lower than those who are willing to shake hands with the powers that be.

text:
the simple man, upon hearing wisdom, understands it and begins to live it. in that moment, it touches his heart. the spirit soon follows, finding empty space to dwell within.

the complicated man, upon hearing wisdom, deconstructs it, questions it, examines it, but never lives it, for by the time he has thought thoroughly about it, he has already lost it, having unknowingly invented an endless number of paths forward, inveigled before his self-constructed mirage. so he continues to strain his teary eyes, searching for the truth among lies, forever weary, endlessly longing, hopelessly aching…

yet the simple path, the meaning of wisdom, softly sings still, just below his level of perception. it is this sound which may finally redeem the complicated man, which may finally give him rest and a new way forward, through the mirage and toward that narrow gate which beckons us all.

text:
I think there is a profound complexity in the simplest of things. For instance, the scent of a rose and her lit, humble beauty is understood intuitively and simply, by the heart. Further inspection of course reveals the complexity of her rhythm and the symmetry of her petals, but even within these complexities we find an equally elegant, simple, and humble beauty.

I find that we benefit from complexity only when our cornerstone is the meek and simple Christ, the Lord of the harvest and the genitor of Creation, whose complexity can never be truly grasped but only experienced, as is the case with any great symphony.

One can be simple and complex, I imagine. ‘The complicated man’, however, is complicated because he views the way toward God as complex, perhaps through the mere study of books and acquisition of knowledge. In reality the Way is as simple as listening to music and embodying its meaning through a sort of dance, a form of worship. We follow God step by step, through the front door, not in a round about way, perhaps through the back or a slightly open window, all means of entry indicative of the fabled ‘complicated man’.

Equally, a man can become ‘complicated’ when he meditates on himself through himself instead of through the Spirit, Who knows His ways better than himself (after all, who can know himself better than He who made him?). This is the mirage which he is trapped in: the pretense that the soul is a labyrinth, with unending chambers and hidden qualities, when in reality it more properly resembles a mustard tree, which is grown in the fertile soil of Christ and watered by humble faith. Only then can its branches reach a design of infinite beauty and complexity, only then does it bear ripe fruit.

Perhaps a bit abstract, but this is how I’ve thought about it.

raw.full_text:
I think there is a profound complexity in the simplest of things. For instance, the scent of a rose and her lit, humble beauty is understood intuitively and simply, by the heart. Further inspection of course reveals the complexity of her rhythm and the symmetry of her petals, but even within these complexities we find an equally elegant, simple, and humble beauty.

I find that we benefit from complexity only when our cornerstone is the meek and simple Christ, the Lord of the harvest and the genitor of Creation, whose complexity can never be truly grasped but only experienced, as is the case with any great symphony.

One can be simple and complex, I imagine. ‘The complicated man’, however, is complicated because he views the way toward God as complex, perhaps through the mere study of books and acquisition of knowledge. In reality the Way is as simple as listening to music and embodying its meaning through a sort of dance, a form of worship. We follow God step by step, through the front door, not in a round about way, perhaps through the back or a slightly open window, all means of entry indicative of the fabled ‘complicated man’.

Equally, a man can become ‘complicated’ when he meditates on himself through himself instead of through the Spirit, Who knows His ways better than himself (after all, who can know himself better than He who made him?). This is the mirage which he is trapped in: the pretense that the soul is a labyrinth, with unending chambers and hidden qualities, when in reality it more properly resembles a mustard tree, which is grown in the fertile soil of Christ and watered by humble faith. Only then can its branches reach a design of infinite beauty and complexity, only then does it bear ripe fruit.

Perhaps a bit abstract, but this is how I’ve thought about it.

text:
im on the phone with a buddy in finance the other day and we start talking about these books he got from a widow after he helped her clean out her husband’s belongings. 

ofc most of the books were mostly on practical matters, but then he goes “oh wait you might think this one is cool” and he then proceeds to send me pictures of a first edition hermetic text with the og dust jacket. 

lo and behold I quickly discover that without the dust jacket, it is valued at $1,000. and he received it for free. to the widow, they were just books she was never going to read, deadweight, or perhaps a sorrow reminder of a love lost. in that sense, i understand.

the contents of the book u can find really in any new age text. rather typical woo-woo stuff, nothing profound. but it’s just so interesting how we can overlook value simply because we are ignorant. equally, my buddy hadn’t even taken it off the shelf once received, nor did he know what hermeticism was. just another facet of existence he was unaware of (perhaps for the better).

makes me wonder about the value we tend to overlook due to our own ignorance. when a man dies, graverobbers look for jewelry and gilded things, overlooking anything that does not immediately glimmer.

i think this happens in our own lives more than we’d like to admit. how many things of immense value do we pass over because they do not immediately appeal to us, because we lack the eyes to see and the ears to hear?

text:
the artist looks up to the intellectual, studying his words and finding their beauty. the intellectual looks up to the artist, standing in awe before his work, finding all sorts of unseen practicalities in lines and curves of sound and image.

the two are mutually beneficial. but they must be careful in their admiration  not to mistake themselves for each other, lest they go mad, and try to write a symphony with theology, or a treatise on a canvas with watercolors.

i imagine that one can dabble in both domains, like Goethe, but even then, one’s natural disposition, particularly if he is eccentric, tends to lean into one side more than the other.

text:
A gentle stream slices a valley out of a mountain. A bullet shot in water comes to a complete stop. The words of a jester saves a kingdom from a despotic king. The mammals inherent a world once ruled by dragons. Belle turned a beast into a prince. A crucified God brought Rome to its knees.

This is why Jesus tells us to turn the other cheek. When we do so properly, we become like a great martial artist, using our enemy's momentum against him, effortlessly. "To be soft" or "to be meek" is by no stretch of the imagination a weakness, but the way reality is.

“If you gathered nothing in your youth, how can you find anything in your old age?”

text:
this little story speaks to me, as I now am 24 and worry about the future and what will become of me if I do not begin ‘gathering’ in earnest, so to speak. I don’t think that means material wealth only, but also other things like building a family, service, creative endeavors and so on like you mentioned.

the fact he turned his life around I think speaks to the ability for us to be able to change regardless of how forgone we may seem, but still we can lose years of life to nothing regardless. that is quite a somber fact. you can’t get those back. the songs of youth cannot be sung in old age.

thank you for this story, it struck a chord with me. let today be the day we do something, if something needs to be done, and let’s pray that we can see life’s gift in all, so that when we grow old, we will have much to find, and much to love.

text: When you go off script, you often find yourself on the correct path.

text:
there is a certain kind of personality i have always found myself at odds with, and that is the thinking type, which i once believed myself to be, but only because others close to me casted such a judgement my way. in truth, i do not think my way through the world, but feel my way, touching everything that i come across, feeling it's textures and judging its weight in the palm of my hand.

undoubtedly, this leads me to grab things preemptively, like a blind man, clutching too tightly something that i know nothing of, and inevitably i am bitten, for my blind eyes that cannot see the bright colors that so obviously reveal the truth of the situation. but it is all i've ever known, to touch and to hear: it has been sight that i've lacked for so long, which is most likely why i wear contacts. so lord give me sight. as much as i like to feel, some things are best appreciated at a distance.

text:
i see sin as a sort of negation, i.e. a privation, of what it means to be fundamentally human. it severs us from vitality and health, both bodily and mentally.

gluttony takes one's humanity by limiting his movement & his capacity to be effectual, lust takes one's humanity by taking away his capacity for true eros (negating the song of songs), pride takes one's humanity by centering himself on himself, forsaking communion with others, Creation, & God (we are social creatures, after all), etc.

text:
i am convinced that screen use suppresses memory. 

whenever i abstain and find myself on a long walk, memories from long ago appear out of thin air, important moments that contain valuable information and emotions that i have forgotten, but must remember in order to move forward.

ofc they were always there, always seeking to be recognized and understood, but they had been continually suppressed by the malefic hypnagogic state that screen use induces.

it creates artificial worlds to take us away from our own, and by design. consequentially, we forget who we are and where we came from. we accept visions, ideas, and identities from entities we know nothing of. we lose our memory, lose our way, and lose our individuality. whether we scroll or post, we do not escape this fact.

yet when we look away and center ourselves on reality, we wake up from the lucid dream that is social media. memories return, and like the drunk who sobers up, one can only wonder where he had been and what had happened during all those years that slipped by.

text:
i think if you follow the right people, you can improve reading comprehension to a certain degree. select accounts post great writing & aphorisms, which can improve our comprehension and lead to reading outside of the platform, but it's safe to say that one's reading comprehension is better served through real books. 

it's easier to comprehend short-form nuggets of wisdom & aphorisms, which have their place and are good in their own right, but far harder to comprehend, let's say, Dostoevsky's Demons or Tolstoy's War and Peace, which require continued attention and contain a level of detail and care that is mostly absent from the short-form writing we see here.

and also between the good writing we encounter on this platform is a whole lot of bad writing, or writing that doesn't come from a place of sincerity, but ideology, possession, etc. this form of writing is dangerous to consume.

text:
Carl Jung is correct in saying that “everything has a shadow”. Within Christianity, this is known as “the world”. Yet he is incorrect in saying that evil is ‘a priori’, that is, innate, for despite his genius, he was unable to see that it was the Fall that cast the first shadow.

Having fallen from grace with Eden, humanity fell into the valley, living in the shadow of darkness, and what was good was obscured.

The gnostics, drunk on their own depravity, clouded their pre-lapsarian memory. They could see that the light sat atop the mountain, but they could not see that that mountain was their origin, that their “process of individuation”, that is, their search for gnosis, was not an attempt at a first ascent of the mountain, which they conquer through their will and inner light, but rather a coming home, the return of the prodigal son, who is guided home by a light outside of himself.

Hence Jung—who always wore his coveted gnostic ring—could not see that evil had no substance, for like Solomon, despite his wisdom he fell victim to the flesh and sigils. His memory was clouded, too, he was unable to see that humanity is fundamentally good, corrupted by the virus that is evil.

The prelapsarian sentiment was lost, and instead of fleeing from evil, he sought to integrate it. Yet who can integrate a virus and remain healthy? Nobody. It is for this reason why Christ is the ultimate physician, for the remedy for sickness is that which is good.

text:
This whole the West vs. Japan pirating issue is very intriguing ethnographically, in the sense that culturally, the divide is principally a consequence of the West’s Christianity.

The West does not disregard the law. In fact our God is the Logos, the law made flesh. We value the law immeasurably.

Yet our God also broke the law in order to restore it. He was subversive to those who upheld the law because they were upholding it unrighteously.

Equally, it is expected that everyone is to have access to the canon, the good news, the Gospels and the scripture. If anything restricts that (i.e. mamon-worshipping corporations), then we will gladly subvert the written law in order to follow the living law, which may or may not agree with what governments have constituted as just.

That is, the law as a cultural symbol in the West is not just about maintaining collective harmony, but principally about the pursuit of God and the salvation of the individual soul.

Thus, when we in the West pirate media, we are deeming that the unwritten law is just, while the written law is unjust, echoing Jesus. However, since the death of God, this “unwritten law” is mediated through the Self alone, as opposed to through the Self in communion with the Logos, which is why piracy is perhaps unjustified in many cases, for:

“Render to Ceaser what is Ceaser’s, and unto God what is God’s”.

Yet our God also broke the law in order to restore it. He was subversive to those who upheld the law because they were upholding it unrighteously.

Equally, it is expected that everyone is to have access to the canon, the good news, the Gospels and the scripture. If anything restricts that (i.e. mamon-worshipping corporations), then we will gladly subvert the written law in order to follow the living law, which may or may not agree with what governments have constituted as just.

That is, the law as a cultural symbol in the West is not just about maintaining collective harmony, but principally about the pursuit of God and the salvation of the individual soul.

Thus, when we in the West pirate media, we are deeming that the unwritten law is just, while the written law is unjust, echoing Jesus. However, since the death of God, this “unwritten law” is mediated through the Self alone, as opposed to through the Self in communion with the Logos, which is why piracy is perhaps unjustified in many cases, for:

“Render to Ceaser what is Ceaser’s, and unto God what is God’s”.

text:
people are generally more interested in feeling right as opposed to being correct. the former inflames pride, while the latter extinguishes it.

there are few feelings better than feeling certain, hence why so many are so certain about the falsehoods they believe in.

but the wise man is only certain he that knows nothing, hence understanding comes easy to him. he clings not to certainty, so it never flees from him. 

in other words, he is certain of his uncertainty and thus is truly certain. he depends not on knowledge alone, but rather its source, and thus can walk through darkness without stumbling.
